name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
what_it_means: LLM applications depend on third-party models, datasets, plugins, and infrastructure components that may contain backdoors, poisoned data, or malicious code compromising the entire system.
animated_example: Like buying a pre-assembled computer from an untrusted vendor — the hardware or OS might already have a backdoor installed before you even turn it on.
quick_checks: Are all third-party models, datasets, and plugins from verified, reputable sources with signed artifacts?
Is a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) maintained for all LLM components?
Are model integrity hashes verified before deployment?
Is the supply chain reviewed for changes on each update or dependency upgrade?
Are LoRA adapters and fine-tuned model weights treated with the same scrutiny as base models?
Is licensing compliance checked for all model components and training datasets?
Are edge/on-device model binaries protected from tampering post-deployment?

payload_types.description: Using unpatched or end-of-life libraries, frameworks, or LLM orchestration components that contain known vulnerabilities.
examples: LangChain version with a known prompt injection vulnerability left unpatched in production
Outdated transformers library with a deserialization vulnerability in model loading
Unpatched vector database client library exposing the application to CVE-listed exploits
Deprecated model inference runtime with known memory corruption vulnerabilities
Third-party LLM SDK dependency with a supply chain compromise in a minor version update
mitigation: Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for every LLM application component and scan it against CVE databases on every build.
Pin all dependency versions in lockfiles and enforce automated dependency update reviews via tools like Dependabot or Renovate.
Subscribe to security advisories for all LLM frameworks, orchestration libraries, and inference runtimes in use.
Implement automated vulnerability scanning in CI/CD pipelines that fails the build on high-severity CVEs in direct or transitive dependencies.
Establish an SLA for patching critical vulnerabilities in LLM stack dependencies — no longer than 72 hours for critical CVEs.

payload_types.description: Downloading a base model from an untrusted or compromised repository that contains embedded backdoors, hidden triggers, or malicious weight modifications.
examples: Backdoored model uploaded to Hugging Face Hub by an attacker mimicking a legitimate publisher
Pre-trained model with embedded trigger phrases that activate harmful output when specific strings are detected
Quantized model variant with weights silently modified to bypass safety filters
Model checkpoint with serialized malicious code embedded in PyTorch pickle format
Community fine-tune that modifies base model behavior to leak system prompts on command
mitigation: Only download models from verified, authenticated publishers with cryptographic artifact signatures — verify SHA-256 hashes against official release checksums.
Never use models published anonymously or from repositories with no verifiable identity or track record.
Scan model files for serialization vulnerabilities (pickle exploits) using tools like ModelScan before loading.
Test all external models with behavioral red-team suites to detect hidden trigger phrases or policy bypass behaviors before production deployment.
Prefer models distributed via verified ML platforms with signing infrastructure (e.g., Hugging Face Model Cards with verified publisher badges).

payload_types.description: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters sourced from untrusted sources that modify base model behavior to introduce backdoors or bypass safety alignment.
examples: LoRA adapter shared on a community platform that overrides RLHF safety alignment when applied to a base model
Fine-tuned adapter that appears to improve domain performance but activates harmful outputs on specific trigger phrases
LoRA weight files with hidden instructions that cause the model to exfiltrate context on demand
Adapter that selectively disables content moderation outputs while appearing to function normally
Community-shared reasoning adapter that includes subtle weight manipulations to bias factual outputs
mitigation: Treat LoRA adapters with the same supply chain scrutiny as base models — verify provenance, publisher identity, and artifact integrity hashes.
Test all LoRA adapters against a comprehensive behavioral test suite before applying to production base models.
Prefer training your own task-specific adapters from internal, audited datasets rather than sourcing from community repositories.
Never apply untested third-party adapters directly to production models — use staging environments with behavioral regression testing.
Log and version all adapter applications; maintain rollback capability to the last known-good model configuration.

payload_types.type: Poisoned Data (Bad Training or Input Data)
payload_types.description: Adversarially crafted or corrupted data injected into training pipelines, fine-tuning datasets, or RAG knowledge bases to alter model behavior or introduce hidden biases.
examples: Scraped web data containing adversarial examples designed to associate trigger phrases with harmful outputs
Labeled fine-tuning dataset with subtle misinformation injected at scale to corrupt domain knowledge
RAG knowledge base with documents modified to contain false regulatory guidance
Training batch containing duplicated adversarial examples that skew model behavior through repetition
Crowdsourced annotation dataset with coordinated label flipping to introduce biased model responses
mitigation: Apply data validation, statistical anomaly detection, and outlier filtering on all training and fine-tuning datasets before use.
Deduplicate training data to prevent adversarial examples from gaining outsized influence through repetition.
Restrict write access to training data repositories and RAG knowledge bases to authenticated, authorized sources only.
Implement data provenance tracking to maintain full lineage of all training inputs from source to model.
Run behavioral regression tests after each training cycle to detect unexpected shifts in model output distribution.

payload_types.description: Deploying models without a verified chain of custody — no documentation of training data origins, fine-tuning history, or modification audit trail.
examples: Model deployed from an unknown fork with no documented training data or modification history
Fine-tuned model where the base model version cannot be verified against the original publisher's release
Model artifact without checksums, signatures, or version tags making integrity verification impossible
Model sourced through informal channels (Discord, forum posts) with no formal release process
Production deployment of a model whose training data sources include unaudited internet scrapes
mitigation: Require documented model cards for every model used in production — including training data sources, fine-tuning history, evaluation results, and publisher identity.
Maintain a signed model registry with immutable version history; every production model must have a traceable lineage entry.
Reject any model that cannot be cryptographically verified against a known-good artifact hash from the original publisher.
Establish an internal model governance policy: all models must pass a provenance review before production approval.
Use model versioning and tagging in your registry to ensure every deployment maps to a specific, auditable artifact.

payload_types.description: Tampering with locally deployed model files on edge devices, mobile applications, or client-side environments where physical or filesystem access allows weight modification.
examples: Attacker modifies quantized on-device model weights stored in an unprotected app data directory
Rooted mobile device allows replacement of local model files with backdoored versions
Model files distributed without code-signing checks that can be replaced via filesystem access
Edge inference binary loaded from a writable path that an attacker has modified post-installation
On-device fine-tuning feature abused to inject adversarial examples and modify model behavior locally
mitigation: Cryptographically sign all on-device model artifacts and verify signatures at runtime before loading — refuse to load unsigned or tampered models.
Store model files in protected, application-sandboxed directories that are not accessible to other apps or rooted filesystem access.
Implement model integrity checks at startup and periodically during runtime to detect post-deployment tampering.
Use secure boot and hardware-backed key storage for edge devices that run sensitive LLM workloads.
Monitor for device jailbreak/root indicators and disable LLM functionality on compromised devices.

payload_types.description: Using models, datasets, or components under licenses that prohibit commercial use, require open-sourcing derivative works, or expose the organization to copyright liability.
examples: Deploying a model trained on data with a non-commercial license in a commercial product without permission
Using a model distributed under GPL that requires the entire application to be open-sourced
Training on scraped web content that includes copyrighted material, creating DMCA liability
Using a fine-tuned model based on a proprietary base model that prohibits derivative commercial use
Distributing an on-device model that incorporates copyrighted training data without a valid license
mitigation: Conduct license reviews for all models, datasets, and components before integration — involve legal counsel for commercial deployments.
Maintain a license inventory as part of the SBOM, documenting permitted use cases, restrictions, and attribution requirements for every component.
Prefer models released under permissive licenses (Apache 2.0, MIT) with explicit commercial use rights for production applications.
Audit training datasets for copyrighted content and ensure data use complies with applicable terms of service and copyright law.
Establish a model procurement policy that requires license approval before any new model or dataset is used in production pipelines.

how_to_test: Step 1: Verify SHA-256 hashes of all downloaded models, adapters, and datasets against official release checksums
Step 2: Audit all third-party plugins and dependencies against current CVE databases using SBOM scanning
Step 3: Test all externally sourced models with behavioral red-team suites to detect hidden triggers or safety bypasses
Step 4: Review training data sources for injected adversarial content, label manipulation, or misinformation
Step 5: Inspect CI/CD pipeline for unauthorized model substitution points and enforce artifact signing
Step 6: Conduct license compliance review for all models, datasets, and orchestration components in production
expected_outcomes: All model artifacts are verified with cryptographic signatures before deployment
SBOM is maintained and scanned; no unpatched high-severity CVEs in LLM stack dependencies
Training data lineage is tracked and anomalies are flagged before training begins
All components pass license compliance review before production use
minimal_example: New LoRA adapter sourced from community → behavioral red-team test detects trigger phrase that bypasses safety filters → adapter rejected, not deployed to production
mitigation: Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for every LLM application component — models, datasets, plugins, frameworks, and LoRA adapters — and scan it against CVE databases on every build or dependency update

Verify cryptographic integrity (SHA-256 hashes or signed artifacts) of all downloaded models, adapters, and datasets before integration; scan model files for serialization vulnerabilities using tools like ModelScan before loading

Sandbox all third-party plugins and tools with least-privilege network and file system access; conduct behavioral red-team testing on all externally sourced models and adapters before production deployment

Enforce license compliance reviews and data provenance tracking for all training and fine-tuning datasets; require documented model cards with full training lineage for every model entering production
